title: A Doctor's Appointment
summary: A patient visits her doctor with concerns about tiredness and headaches. The doctor asks questions, suggests possible causes, and recommends next steps including blood tests and lifestyle changes.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: Good morning. Please come in and take a seat. How can I help you today?

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: Good morning, Doctor. I've been feeling quite tired lately, more than usual. And I've been getting headaches almost every day for the past two weeks.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: I see. How would you describe the headaches? Are they sharp, dull, or throbbing?

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: They're mostly dull and heavy, mainly around my forehead and temples. They don't tend to last more than a few hours, but they keep coming back.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: And the tiredness — is it constant, or do you notice it more at certain times of day?

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: It's pretty much constant. Even after a full night's sleep, I wake up feeling exhausted. I find it hard to concentrate at work.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: Have you had any changes in your diet recently? Or have you been under more stress than usual?

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: I've been skipping lunch quite a lot because I've been busy at work. And yes, there has been a lot of stress recently — we've had a big project deadline coming up.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: That could certainly be contributing to both the headaches and the fatigue. Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar, which often causes headaches and difficulty concentrating.

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: I hadn't thought of that. I assumed it might be something more serious.

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: It could be, which is why I'd like to arrange some blood tests to rule out things like anaemia or a thyroid issue. But I suspect the lifestyle factors are a significant part of the problem.

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: What kind of changes would you recommend?

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: First, make sure you eat regular meals — three a day at consistent times. Try to reduce your caffeine intake, especially in the afternoon. And aim for at least seven to eight hours of sleep.

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: I do drink quite a lot of coffee. Could that be making the headaches worse?

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: Yes, especially if you miss a cup when you're used to having several a day. Caffeine withdrawal is a very common cause of headaches.

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: That makes a lot of sense. I'll definitely try to cut down. When should I come back to discuss the blood test results?

body.speaker: Doctor
body.text: The results should be ready in about five days. Book an appointment at reception for next week and we'll go through everything together.

body.speaker: Patient
body.text: Thank you very much, Doctor. I feel much better just knowing there's a plan.

annotations.meaningVi: nhức nhối, đập theo nhịp đau
annotations.exampleEn: He had a throbbing headache after staring at the screen all day.
She rubbed her temples to relieve the tension headache.
The constant noise from the construction site made it impossible to sleep.
Skipping meals can lead to low energy and difficulty focusing.

annotations.meaningVi: góp phần vào, là nguyên nhân của
annotations.exampleEn: Several factors are contributing to the rise in obesity rates.
Chronic fatigue can seriously affect a person's quality of life.

annotations.meaningVi: hạ đường huyết, lượng đường trong máu thấp
annotations.exampleEn: Low blood sugar can cause dizziness, irritability, and headaches.

annotations.meaningVi: loại trừ khả năng, xác nhận không phải
annotations.exampleEn: The doctor ordered a scan to rule out anything serious.
Iron deficiency is one of the most common causes of anaemia.
An underactive thyroid can cause fatigue, weight gain, and depression.

annotations.meaningVi: các yếu tố lối sống, thói quen sinh hoạt
annotations.exampleEn: Lifestyle factors such as diet and exercise have a big impact on heart health.
Reducing your caffeine intake can improve sleep quality significantly.

annotations.meaningVi: hội chứng thiếu caffeine, phản ứng khi ngừng uống caffeine đột ngột
annotations.exampleEn: Caffeine withdrawal can cause headaches and irritability for a few days.
Her doctor advised her to cut down on salt and processed foods.

annotations.meaningVi: xem xét tất cả cùng nhau, thảo luận toàn bộ vấn đề cùng nhau
annotations.exampleEn: The financial adviser said we'd go through everything together at the next meeting.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect Continuous — Describing ongoing symptoms
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn được dùng để mô tả các triệu chứng đã bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn đang tiếp diễn. Ví dụ: "I've been feeling quite tired lately", "I've been getting headaches almost every day", "I've been skipping lunch". Cấu trúc: have/has + been + V-ing. Thì này nhấn mạnh sự liên tục và khoảng thời gian diễn ra của hành động, rất phổ biến khi mô tả sức khỏe trong bối cảnh y tế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect continuous is the natural tense for describing ongoing symptoms in a medical context. It connects the past (when the symptom started) to the present (it is still happening). 'I've been feeling tired' suggests a duration that has built up over time, making it more informative than simple past 'I felt tired.' This tense signals to the doctor that the problem is not a one-off event.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional Type 1 — Medical recommendations and consequences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 1 được dùng để đưa ra khuyến nghị y tế và dự đoán hậu quả. Ví dụ: "Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar, which often causes headaches" và "if you miss a cup when you're used to having several a day" (hiểu ngầm: bạn sẽ bị đau đầu). 'Can' trong câu điều kiện loại 1 diễn đạt khả năng xảy ra, không phải kết quả chắc chắn. Bác sĩ dùng loại câu này để giải thích và khuyến nghị một cách thận trọng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Type 1 conditionals appear in medical advice to describe likely consequences: 'Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar.' The modal 'can' (rather than 'will') acknowledges that this is a probable, not inevitable, consequence. This softening is appropriate in medical consultations where doctors avoid absolute statements. 'If you miss a cup... caffeine withdrawal causes headaches' is an implicit Type 1 conditional.

translationVi:
Bác sĩ: Chào buổi sáng. Mời vào và ngồi xuống. Hôm nay tôi có thể giúp gì cho bạn?
Bệnh nhân: Chào buổi sáng, Bác sĩ. Dạo này tôi cảm thấy khá mệt mỏi, hơn bình thường. Và tôi bị đau đầu gần như mỗi ngày trong hai tuần qua.
Bác sĩ: Tôi hiểu rồi. Bạn có thể mô tả các cơn đau đầu đó như thế nào? Chúng sắc, âm ỉ hay nhức nhối?
Bệnh nhân: Chủ yếu là âm ỉ và nặng nề, chủ yếu ở vùng trán và thái dương. Thường không kéo dài quá vài tiếng, nhưng cứ tái đi tái lại.
Bác sĩ: Và sự mệt mỏi — nó liên tục hay bạn thấy nhiều hơn vào những thời điểm nhất định trong ngày?
Bệnh nhân: Gần như liên tục. Ngay cả sau một đêm ngủ đủ giấc, tôi vẫn thức dậy cảm thấy kiệt sức. Tôi khó tập trung làm việc.
Bác sĩ: Gần đây bạn có thay đổi gì trong chế độ ăn không? Hay bạn đang chịu nhiều căng thẳng hơn bình thường?
Bệnh nhân: Tôi hay bỏ bữa trưa vì bận công việc. Và đúng, gần đây có rất nhiều căng thẳng — chúng tôi đang có deadline dự án lớn sắp đến.
Bác sĩ: Điều đó chắc chắn có thể góp phần gây ra cả đau đầu và mệt mỏi. Bỏ bữa có thể dẫn đến hạ đường huyết, thường gây ra đau đầu và khó tập trung.
Bệnh nhân: Tôi chưa nghĩ đến điều đó. Tôi đã nghĩ có thể là gì đó nghiêm trọng hơn.
Bác sĩ: Có thể là, đó là lý do tôi muốn sắp xếp một số xét nghiệm máu để loại trừ những vấn đề như thiếu máu hoặc tuyến giáp. Nhưng tôi nghi ngờ các yếu tố lối sống là phần quan trọng của vấn đề.
Bệnh nhân: Bạn sẽ khuyến nghị những thay đổi gì?
Bác sĩ: Đầu tiên, đảm bảo bạn ăn đều đặn — ba bữa một ngày vào các giờ nhất quán. Cố gắng giảm lượng caffeine tiêu thụ, đặc biệt vào buổi chiều. Và đặt mục tiêu ngủ ít nhất bảy đến tám tiếng.
Bệnh nhân: Tôi uống khá nhiều cà phê. Điều đó có thể làm đau đầu tệ hơn không?
Bác sĩ: Có, đặc biệt nếu bạn bỏ một tách khi đã quen uống vài tách mỗi ngày. Cai caffeine là nguyên nhân rất phổ biến gây đau đầu.
Bệnh nhân: Nghe có lý lắm. Tôi chắc chắn sẽ cố gắng giảm bớt. Khi nào tôi nên quay lại để thảo luận kết quả xét nghiệm máu?
Bác sĩ: Kết quả sẽ có trong khoảng năm ngày. Đặt lịch hẹn ở quầy tiếp tân cho tuần sau và chúng ta sẽ xem xét mọi thứ cùng nhau.
Bệnh nhân: Cảm ơn Bác sĩ rất nhiều. Tôi cảm thấy tốt hơn nhiều khi biết đã có kế hoạch.

questions.prompt: What two main symptoms does the patient describe?
questions.explanation: The patient says 'I've been feeling quite tired lately' and 'I've been getting headaches almost every day for the past two weeks.'
questions.hint: The patient mentions both symptoms in her first turn.

questions.prompt: How does the patient describe the type of headaches she is having?
options: Sharp and sudden, lasting all day
Mild and occasional, mostly in the evenings
Dull and heavy, mainly around the forehead and temples
Throbbing and severe, often causing nausea
questions.explanation: The patient says the headaches are 'mostly dull and heavy, mainly around my forehead and temples.'
questions.hint: Think about the location and quality of the pain described.

questions.prompt: What does the patient say about her sleep?
options: She often struggles to fall asleep
She wakes up exhausted even after a full night's sleep
She is sleeping too much and feels groggy
She has been having vivid nightmares
questions.explanation: The patient says 'Even after a full night's sleep, I wake up feeling exhausted.'
questions.hint: The issue is not the amount of sleep but how the patient feels after sleeping.

questions.prompt: What lifestyle factor does the patient admit to the doctor?
options: She has been exercising too much
She has been drinking alcohol every evening
She has been skipping lunch because of work
She has been going to bed very late
questions.explanation: The patient says 'I've been skipping lunch quite a lot because I've been busy at work.'
questions.hint: The patient admits to a habit related to eating.

questions.prompt: Why does the doctor say skipping meals can cause headaches?
options: Because it leads to low blood sugar
Because it disrupts the digestive system
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar, which often causes headaches and difficulty concentrating.'
questions.hint: Think about what happens to blood sugar levels when you do not eat.

questions.prompt: What tests does the doctor want to arrange?
options: A brain scan and an eye test
Blood pressure monitoring and a urine test
Blood tests to rule out anaemia or a thyroid issue
An MRI scan and a hearing test
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'I'd like to arrange some blood tests to rule out things like anaemia or a thyroid issue.'
questions.hint: The doctor wants to eliminate specific medical conditions as causes.

questions.prompt: What dietary change does the doctor recommend regarding caffeine?
options: Switch from coffee to herbal tea
Reduce caffeine intake, especially in the afternoon
Drink more water between cups of coffee
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'Try to reduce your caffeine intake, especially in the afternoon.'
questions.hint: The doctor does not tell her to stop entirely, just to reduce.

questions.prompt: According to the doctor, why can missing a cup of coffee cause a headache?
options: Because coffee contains vitamins that the body needs
Because caffeine withdrawal is a very common cause of headaches
Because coffee dehydrates the body when consumed regularly
Because caffeine helps blood vessels to relax
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'Caffeine withdrawal is a very common cause of headaches' — this happens when someone is used to coffee and misses their usual amount.
questions.hint: The issue is not coffee itself but the sudden absence of it.

questions.prompt: How many hours of sleep does the doctor recommend?
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'aim for at least seven to eight hours of sleep.'
questions.hint: The doctor gives a specific range.

questions.prompt: When will the blood test results be ready?
questions.explanation: The doctor says 'The results should be ready in about five days.'
questions.hint: The doctor gives an approximate timeframe for the results.

questions.prompt: How does the patient feel at the end of the appointment?
options: Worried about what the blood tests might reveal
Frustrated that the doctor did not prescribe medication
Better just from knowing there is a plan
Relieved that her condition is not serious
questions.explanation: The patient says 'I feel much better just knowing there's a plan.'
questions.hint: The patient's emotional state is described in her final statement.

cloze.template: Patient: I've been feeling quite tired lately, more than usual. And I've been getting headaches almost every day for the past two weeks. Doctor: How would you describe the headaches? Are they sharp, dull, or {{b1}}? Patient: They're mostly dull and heavy, mainly around my forehead and {{b2}}. Doctor: And the tiredness — is it {{b3}}, or do you notice it more at certain times of day? Patient: It's pretty much constant. Even after a full night's sleep, I wake up feeling {{b4}}. Doctor: Have you been under more stress than usual? Patient: I've been {{b5}} lunch quite a lot because I've been busy at work. And there has been a lot of stress recently. Doctor: That could certainly be {{b6}} to both the headaches and the {{b7}}. Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar, which often causes headaches. Doctor: I'd like to arrange some blood tests to {{b8}} things like {{b9}} or a thyroid issue. Patient: What changes would you recommend? Doctor: Make sure you eat regular meals. Try to reduce your {{b10}} intake, especially in the afternoon. And aim for at least seven to eight hours of sleep. Patient: I'll definitely try to {{b11}}. Thank you very much, Doctor.

blanks.explanation: 'Throbbing' is a medical term for pain that beats in a rhythm, like a pulse — one of three headache types the doctor lists.
'Temples' are the flat sides of the head between the forehead and ear — a common location for tension headaches.
'Constant' means uninterrupted and without variation — the patient uses this word to describe her tiredness.
'Exhausted' means completely drained of energy — a stronger word than 'tired' that conveys the severity of the patient's fatigue.
'Skipping meals' is the standard collocation for not eating a meal on purpose or by accident — the phrase used in the passage.
'Contributing to' means being one of several factors that cause or worsen something — an appropriate medical expression.
'Fatigue' is the medical term for persistent tiredness — the doctor uses clinical vocabulary rather than the everyday 'tiredness.'
'Rule out' means to eliminate a possibility through testing — standard medical phraseology when ordering diagnostic tests.
'Anaemia' is a condition where the blood lacks enough red blood cells — a common cause of tiredness and headaches.
'Caffeine intake' refers to how much caffeine a person consumes — the doctor's specific recommendation about reducing it.
'Cut down' is an informal phrasal verb meaning to reduce the amount of something consumed — here referring to coffee.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The doctor suggests that lifestyle factors — diet, caffeine, and stress — are likely contributing to the patient's symptoms before receiving the test results. Do you think doctors should discuss lifestyle changes before test results are available, or should they wait for a confirmed diagnosis first? What are the advantages and disadvantages of each approach?

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: Good morning. Please come in and take a seat. How can I help you today?

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: Good morning, Doctor. I've been feeling quite tired lately, more than usual. And I've been getting headaches almost every day for the past two weeks.

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: I see. How would you describe the headaches? Are they sharp, dull, or throbbing?

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: They're mostly dull and heavy, mainly around my forehead and temples. They don't tend to last more than a few hours, but they keep coming back.

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: And the tiredness — is it constant, or do you notice it more at certain times of day?

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: It's pretty much constant. Even after a full night's sleep, I wake up feeling exhausted. I find it hard to concentrate at work.

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: Have you had any changes in your diet recently? Or have you been under more stress than usual?

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: I've been skipping lunch quite a lot because I've been busy at work. And yes, there has been a lot of stress recently — we've had a big project deadline coming up.

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: That could certainly be contributing to both the headaches and the fatigue. Skipping meals can lead to low blood sugar, which often causes headaches and difficulty concentrating.

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: I hadn't thought of that. I assumed it might be something more serious.

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: It could be, which is why I'd like to arrange some blood tests to rule out things like anaemia or a thyroid issue. But I suspect the lifestyle factors are a significant part of the problem.

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: What kind of changes would you recommend?

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: First, make sure you eat regular meals — three a day at consistent times. Try to reduce your caffeine intake, especially in the afternoon. And aim for at least seven to eight hours of sleep.

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: I do drink quite a lot of coffee. Could that be making the headaches worse?

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: Yes, especially if you miss a cup when you're used to having several a day. Caffeine withdrawal is a very common cause of headaches.

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: That makes a lot of sense. I'll definitely try to cut down. When should I come back to discuss the blood test results?

sentences.speaker: Doctor
sentences.text: The results should be ready in about five days. Book an appointment at reception for next week and we'll go through everything together.

sentences.speaker: Patient
sentences.text: Thank you very much, Doctor. I feel much better just knowing there's a plan.
